entries.ocr_text:
PROCLAMATION
WHEREAS , It has become imperative that the General As-
sembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky enact legislation prior
to January 1, 1937 , providing for cooperation with the Federal
Government in the matter of unemployment compensation insur-
ance ; and
WHEREAS , Certain other emergencies affecting the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky have arisen , including the need for re-
pealing the Omnibus Tax Bill, House Bill 59 , Third Extraordinary
Session 1936 :
NOW , THEREFORE , In order to enact legislation providing
for cooperation with the Federal Government in the matter of
unemployment compensation insurance , and in order to repeal
the Omnibus Tax Bill , House Bill 59 , Third Extraordinary Ses-
sion , 1936 , and to permit the consideration of certain other emer-
gencies which have arisen , and according to the power vested in
me by the Constitution of Kentucky to convene the General As-
sembly into extraordinary session , I do issue this my proclama-
tion convening the General Assembly of Kentucky in extraor-
dinary session at the seat of government at Frankfort , Ken-
tucky , at one o'clock p . m ., on Wednesday , December 23 , 1936 , for
the purpose of considering the following subjects :
1. To enact unemployment compensation legislation and to
establish a State employment service so as to enable the State
of Kentucky to come within the purview of the Federal Social
Security Act and secure to employees in Kentucky industry the
protection contemplated by the provisions of unemployment com-
pensation titles of said Social Security Act .
2. To provide an allowance to compensate tobacco whole-
salers for stamping cigarettes under the terms of Chapter four
of the Third Extraordinary Session of the General Assembly of
1936 , and to require cigarette manufacturers to submit reports.
3. To pass an act to provide for collection of omitted and
delinquent taxes , and to compel collecting officers to transmit
monies in their charge to the treasury of the Commonwealth .
4. To provide for repeal of Chapter three of the Acts of the
Third Extraordinary Session of the General Assembly of 1936 ,
and to continue in effect the administrative provisions neces-
sary to collect taxes on account of sales made ( and , in case of
cosmetics , of merchandise received ) prior to the effective date
of the repealing act .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, Extraordinary Session, 1937, p. 16

classification.title: Proclamation Convening General Assembly in Extraordinary Session
classification.summary: This proclamation convenes the Kentucky General Assembly into an extraordinary session on December 23, 1936, to address several urgent matters. These include enacting legislation for unemployment compensation insurance to comply with the Federal Social Security Act, providing compensation to tobacco wholesalers for stamping cigarettes, collecting omitted and delinquent taxes, and repealing a specific chapter of a previous act while maintaining administrative provisions for tax collection.
classification.reasoning: The document is a proclamation calling for an extraordinary legislative session. The stated purposes of the session are to enact unemployment compensation legislation, provide allowances to tobacco wholesalers, and address tax collection issues. None of these purposes inherently relate to racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly outlines the legislative agenda, which is economic and administrative in nature, with no mention or implication of race.

chasing power is maintained the reduced power of tobacco pro-
ducers to purchase industrial products seriously impairs the

classification.title: Gilbert-Wells Tobacco Control Act

It outlines the powers and duties of the commission, local committeemen, and employees. It declares an emergency and is known as the Gilbert-Wells Tobacco Control Act.

11. (a ) Each corporation formed hereunder
shall have a board of directors of not less than five
members which board shall constitute the governing
body of the corporation . Unless otherwise provided
in the articles of incorporation , or an amendment
thereof , it shall not be necessary for directors to be
members . Subject to the provisions of the articles

business and affairs of the corporation . The direc-
tors of the corporation , other than those named in
its articles of incorporation , shall be elected an-
nually or as provided for in the by-laws, provided ,
however , that no director shall be elected for a
longer term than three years , by the members en-
titled to vote therefor . The directors shall be en-
titled to such compensation and reimbursement of
expenses incurred by them as may be provided in
the by-laws.
(b) When a vancy on the board of directors oc-
curs other than by expiration of the term, the re-
maining members of the board, by a majority vote ,
shall fill the vacancy , unless the by-laws otherwise
provide .
( c ) The board of directors may adopt rules and
regulations governing the procedure of the board.
and of the operations of the corporation formed
hereunder which shall not be inconsistent with the
articles of incorporation or the by-laws .

thereby .
§ 31. All acts and parts of acts and all statutory
provisions and all laws and parts of laws in con-
flict with the provisions of this act or any part of
this act are hereby expressly repealed .
§ 32. Whereas , many Rural Electric Coopera-
tive Corporations are now ready to be organized
and delay in their organization will endanger their
receiving the federal funds appropriated for that
purpose , an emergency is hereby declared to exist
and this Act shall become a law and be effective
from and after its passage and approval by the
Governor .
Approved January 18 , 1937 .

CHAPTER 7 55
cation of this Act , the public policy of this State
is declared to be as follows :
Economic insecurity due to unemployment is a
serious menace to the health , morals and welfare
of the people of this State . Involuntary unemploy-
ment is therefore a subject of general interest and
concern which requires appropriate action by the
General Assembly to prevent its spread and to
lighten its burden which now so often falls with
crushing force upon the unemployed worker and his
family . The achievement of social security requires
protection against this greatest hazard of our
economic life . This can be provided by encouraging
employers to provide more stable employment and
by the systematic accumulation of funds during
periods of employment to provide benefits for
periods of unemployment , thus maintaining pur-
chasing power and limiting the serious social con-
sequences of poor relief assistance . The General
Assembly, therefore , declares that in its considered
judgment the public good , and the general welfare
of the citizens of this State require the enactment
of this measure , under the police powers of the
State , for the compulsory setting aside of unemploy-
ment reserves to be used for the benefit of persons
unemployed through no fault of their own .
3. BENEFITS . (a) Payment of Benefits .
Twenty -four months after the date when contribu-
tions ( as defined in Section 19 ( d ) ) first accrue under
this Act from any employer (as defined in Section
19 ( f ) ) , benefits shall become payable to any indivi-
dual who thereafter is or becomes unemployed and
eligible for benefits . All benefits shall be paid
through the employment offices , or such other agency
as the Social Security Board may approve , at such

classification.summary: This statute declares the public policy of Kentucky regarding economic insecurity due to unemployment. It is enacted under the state's police powers for the compulsory setting aside of unemployment reserves for individuals unemployed through no fault of their own. Benefits are to be paid through employment offices or approved agencies.

CHAPTER 7 61
§ 5. DISQUALIFICATION FOR BENEFITS . An indi-
vidual shall be ineligible for benefits :
(a ) For the week in which he has left work volun-
tarily without good cause or has been discharged
for misconduct and for not less than two nor more
than the nine next following weeks , ( in addition to
the waiting period ) , as determined by the Commis-
sion in each case according to the circumstances of
the case.
(b ) If an otherwise eligible individual fails , with-
out good cause , either to apply for suitable work
when notified by the employment office , or to accept
suitable work when offered him or return to his cus-
tomary self-employment , if any , he shall thereby
become ineligible for benefits for the week in which
such failure occurred and for not less than two nor
more than the nine next following weeks (in addi-
tion to the waiting period ) as determined by the
Commission .
" Suitable work " shall mean any work for which
the individual in question is reasonably fitted , which
is located within a reasonable distance of his resi-
dence or last employment , and which is not detri-
mental to his health , safety or morals .
Notwithstanding any other provision of this Act ,
no work shall be deemed suitable , and benefits shall
not be denied under this Act to any otherwise eligi-
ble individual for refusing to accept new work under
any of the following conditions : ( a ) If the position
offered is vacant due directly to a strike , lockout , or
other labor dispute ; (b ) If the wages , hours , or
other conditions of the work offered are substantial-
ly less favorable to the individual than those pre-
vailing for similar work in the locality ; ( c ) If as
condition of being employed the individual would be
required to join a company union or to resign from

CHAPTER 7 63
§ 6. CLAIM FOR BENEFITS . ( a ) Filing . Claims
for benefits shall be made in accordance with such
regulations as the Commission may prescribe , at the
employment office nearest either to the individual's
place of residence or to the place of his most recent
employment . An employer shall if requested give
to each of his employees at the time such employee
becomes totally unemployed , a printed statement of
the regulations prescribed by the Commission relat-
ing to the filing of claims for benefits . Such printed
statements shall be supplied by the Executive Direc-
tor to each employer without cost to him.
(b ) Initial Determination . A deputy or repre-
sentative designated by the Executive Director , and
hereinafter referred to as a deputy , shall promptly
examine the claim and , on the basis of the facts
found by him , may determine whether or not such
claim is valid , and if valid , the week with respect
to which benefits shall commence , the weekly benefit
amount payable and the maximum duration thereof,
or may refer such claim to the Commission , which
shall make the same determination with respect
thereto in accordance with the procedure prescribed
in subsection ( e ) of this section . He shall promptly
notify the claimant and the employer or employers
against whose reserve account such benefits are
chargeable , of the decision and the reasons therefor .
Unless the claimant or such employer or employers
within seven calendar days after the delivery of
such notification , or within seven calendar days after
such notification was mailed by registered mail to
his last known address , files with the Commission
an appeal from such decision and applies for a hear-
ing , such decision shall be final and benefits shall be
paid or denied in accordance therewith . In the event
of an appeal no benefits shall be paid until the final
determination of that appeal by the last body to

ing or from producing books , papers , correspond-

otherwise , except that such witness so testifying
shall not be exempt from prosecution and punish-
ment for perjury committed in so testifying .
(i) Witness Fees . Witnesses subpoenaed pur-
suant to this section shall be allowed fees at a rate
fixed by the Commission . Such fees and all ex-
penses of proceedings involving disputed claims
shall be deemed a part of the expense of administer-
ing this Act.
(j ) Appeal to Courts . Any decision of the Com-
mission , in the absence of an appeal therefrom as
herein provided , shall become final twenty days
after the date thereof, and judicial review thereof
shall be permitted only after any party claiming to
be aggrieved thereby has exhausted his remedies
before the Commission , as provided by this Act .

caped entirely the payment of said tax ; and

§ 10. All laws or parts of laws in conflict here-
with to the extent of such conflict hereby are
repealed .
§ 11. Whereas there is great confusion existing
in the law due to the abolition of the offices of Rev-
enue Agent , and whereas there is no adequate
means by which the Commonwealth , its counties ,
school districts or other taxing districts are pro-
tected and enabled to assess omitted property and
collect delinquent taxes which are daily becoming
barred by the Statute of Limitation , and whereas
there is now insufficient revenue to meet the needs
of the Commonwealth and its agencies , the counties ,
school districts and other taxing districts , an emer-
gency is hereby declared to exist and this Act shall
become a law and be effective immediately upon its
passage and approval by the Governor .
Approved January 18 , 1937.

Be it Resolved by the General Assembly of the

§ 1. That the State Librarian is hereby author-
ized and directed to purchase at not exceeding two
dollars per copy five hundred fifty copies of the book
entitled " Kentucky Directory 1936 " for the use of
the Courts , State and County officers and members
of the General Assembly and such officers as may be

Houses a copy of same , and will furnish the state of-

each county in the state to be kept in his office . The
Librarian will furnish to the Librarian of each state
and territory of the union a copy of said book in
exchange for similar books , for such states and ter-
ritories , and a copy to the Librarian of Congress ,
and deliver to the Secretary of state fifty copies to
exchange . The expense to be paid out of the general
revenue fund .

have the books herein provided for , an emergency
is declared and this resolution shall take effect upon
its approval by the Governor .

WHEREAS , Rough River , has been used for a navigation and ,
Whereas , it is now being used for navigation purposes and said

Kentucky Statutes , it is necessary to obtain the consent of the
Secretary of War before any bridge can be constructed over
the navigable portions of said river and , Whereas , Ohio County
is desirous of moving a bridge from one location on said river
to a location at or near Carters ' Ferry on said Rough River ,
this place being six miles from the former location of said
bridge and , Whereas , it is desired by Ohio County that per-
mission of the Secretary of War be given for the construction
and erection of said bridge at said place , and , Whereas , the
erection of said bridge will in no way obstruct or hinder navi-
gation on said river , Now
Be it Resolved by the Senate of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky , the House of Representa-
tives concurring therein :

D. C. , as provided in Section eight hundred fifty-
three a- one ( 853a - 1 ) of the Kentucky Statutes ,
grant permission to Ohio County to construct and
relocate a highway bridge at Carters Ferry across
Rough River , and that the permission be granted to
erect said bridge at said place .
